part of the CET governing the project priorities
Competitors__c is blank though SAP Ariba, JAGGAER and Sirion were all named on the 16 Sep 2025 call

sources: Avoma call transcript, Exploration call ZYCUS<>Chemours, 16 Sep 2025 (Timothy Duprey)
metrics.narrative: Timothy Duprey quantified the size of the opportunity himself: asked how large Chemours' tail spend was, he answered 'it's probably 80 to a 100,000,000,' which the rep mapped to an estimated $4-8M in savings via the ANA negotiation agent (call, 16 Sep 2025). That is a real, buyer-voiced number, but it has not been validated with anyone who owns budget or a business case, and no formal ROI exercise has occurred.

sources: Salesforce Task history (7 outbound emails, 27 Oct 2025 – 7 Jul 2026, zero logged replies)
champion.narrative: Timothy Duprey explicitly disclaimed the champion role on the call: 'I'd only say that I'd be a stakeholder in it. I'm not driving anything' (16 Sep 2025). He has not initiated any contact since; every one of the seven follow-ups since has come from the rep with no reply. A synthesis-source CRM field nominally names a titled executive (the company's Chief Procurement Officer) as champion, but that person has never engaged on any logged call, task, or email for this opportunity — treated as unconfirmed.

competition.narrative: Three incumbent systems were named directly by the buyer on the 16 Sep 2025 call: SAP Ariba (P2P/transactional only, integration stalled by the paused S/4HANA migration), JAGGAER (used for RFx/RFI/RFQ since Ariba is underused there), and Sirion (contract management, described as having weak dashboards and a fragmented user experience). No active competitive bid or bake-off exists; there is no formal project yet to compete in. Salesforce's own Why_not_Zycus__c field notes 'internal IT / ERP roadmap constraints' as the standing risk, directly consistent with the S/4HANA-pause dynamic the buyer described.

identify_pain.narrative: This is the strongest element on the deal. Timothy Duprey articulated specific, credible pain on the 16 Sep 2025 call: 'poor end to end visibility across direct and indirect spend and contracts'; persistent off-contract ('Maverick') buying despite SAP Ariba P2P due to 'poor catalog governance'; supplier risk/compliance gaps (environmental testing, sustainability); an SAP Ariba integration and RFx gap caused by the paused S/4HANA migration ('any progress at that time slowed or stopped'); and contract-management fragmentation via Sirion, which he confirmed 'doesn't give you the dashboards... an effective dashboard.' Salesforce's own Customer_Business_Problem__c field adds account-level context (9,000+ suppliers across 60+ countries, an active Responsible Procurement/EcoVadis program) consistent with this picture, though that field is a CRM note rather than a buyer quote.

sources: Salesforce Opportunity record (Qualified stage, no paper-process fields populated)
paper_process.narrative: Not reached. No contracting artifact of any kind exists; the deal has not moved past a single exploratory conversation and no formal project has even been chartered by the buyer's own account.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer has engaged on any call, task, or email tied to this opportunity. Salesforce's own Executive_Sponsor_Identified__c and Decision_Maker_Identified__c flags are both False. The sole engaged contact told the rep directly, 'I'm the only stakeholder... I'm not the project leader, part of the CET governing the project priorities' (call, 16 Sep 2025). A synthesis-source CRM field nominally names a titled company executive (the company's CFO) as a 'sponsor,' but that person has never appeared in any call, task, or email logged against this opportunity — treated here as unconfirmed, not as evidence of engagement.

decision_process.narrative: The buyer described an internal group ('CET') that governs project priorities but named no members, cadence, or approval path: 'I'm the only stakeholder... part of the CET governing the project priorities' (call, 16 Sep 2025). No purchase-process fields are populated in Salesforce, and no second Chemours contact has surfaced in the ~10 months since to clarify how this group actually decides.

decision_criteria.narrative: No formal evaluation criteria exist. The account is at the 'trying to understand what may come down the pipeline' stage per the sole contact's own framing (call, 16 Sep 2025); Salesforce's Business_Requirements__c and Top_Challenges_Priorities__c fields are both blank, and no scoping or requirements-gathering session has occurred.

critical_signals.text: The systems Chemours actually runs today — SAP Ariba for transactional purchasing, JAGGAER for RFx, and Sirion for contracts — remain fully in place with no formal replacement project chartered, so staying with the current stack is still the most likely outcome.

The only Chemours contact ever engaged told the rep directly that he isn't running any potential project and could only guess a formal initiative might start 'maybe 2026, we'll see' — and no one closer to that decision has engaged since.
The one concrete technical interest the buyer raised beyond general pain was supplier-specific punch-out catalog capability for tail-spend suppliers, distinct from generic hosted catalogs.

confidence_signals.summary: Buyer engagement has been completely one-directional for roughly ten months: seven outbound emails from the deal owner have drawn zero logged replies since the single 16 Sep 2025 call.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 16 Sep 2025, 294 days old, 185 days to close (Jan 2027). Time in Qualified stage unknown due to missing submission dates. Last activity 13 days ago (24 Jun 2026). The close date implies a mid-cycle commercial stage, yet the deal has no identified economic buyer, no decision process, no champion, and the sole contact explicitly stated he is not driving the project. A qualified deal 185 days from close with no buyer-side project structure is indefensible. Required pace to reach Vendor Selected by Q4 2026 (to support Jan 2027 close) demands forming a buying committee, mapping the CET governance group, and securing executive sponsorship within 60 days. Current trajectory shows no such velocity.
evidence: Single-threaded: only Timothy Duprey engaged, who stated 'I'm not the project driver' (discovery call 12 Mar 2026)

No charter, no budget confirmed, no procurement mandate

Forecast category Pipeline with 185 days to close is appropriate only if this were genuinely qualified; current evidence shows it is pre-discovery
north_star_verdict.headline: Qualified stage with 185 days to close but no project sponsor, no budget, no charter, and a single non-driver contact. This is discovery disguised as a qualified pipeline deal.

flags: Single-threaded on a stated non-decision-maker for ~10 months; converting Timothy Duprey's interest into a real deal requires reaching the CET sponsor who actually governs the 2026 project he referenced.
No compelling event or funded charter exists yet; quantify the $4-8M savings opportunity Tim already validated so it becomes a business case the CET can act on.

No formal competitive review has started, giving Zycus a quiet window to pre-position against the fragmented Ariba/Jaggaer/Sirion stack before Chemours opens a wider evaluation that could include new vendors.
Multi-thread beyond Timothy Duprey: he is a self-described non-driver, so identify and engage the internal group's actual project leader before investing further in this single contact.

best_practice_check.summary: Two practices dominate at this stage: break the single-thread pattern, and clean up the contact-role gap so future sweeps don't start from a blank stakeholder picture.

customer_preference.evidence: No buyer-voiced preference for Zycus has been captured. Timothy Duprey engaged with genuine interest in the demo content on 16 Sep 2025 but made no statement of intent to select or prefer Zycus, and has not re-engaged since.

competitive_position.summary: No active competitive evaluation is underway. The three vendors discussed are Chemours' own incumbent systems — SAP Ariba, JAGGAER, and Sirion — each with buyer-acknowledged gaps, but with no formal replacement project chartered by the buyer's internal CET group, the strongest competing outcome right now is inertia: the incumbents stay in place while the CET decides whether to form a project at all.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: any progress at that time slowed or stopped... so now we're back and looking at, you know, the management portal of using Ariba with mainly a transactional level
competitors.how_we_win: Position the unified suite's deeper coverage (RFx, supplier risk, contract visibility) as solving what Ariba alone hasn't, independent of the stalled S/4HANA timeline.

competitors.name: JAGGAER
competitors.quote: we use YAGGER YAGGER for that or RFIs and RFRFQs and data gathering
competitors.how_we_win: Show how a single platform removes the need to split sourcing/RFx work across a second tool.

competitors.name: Sirion
competitors.quote: it doesn't give you the dashboards... you don't get an effective dashboard to, at a glance, give you enough of the collaborative quick glance information
competitors.how_we_win: Lead with the AI-driven clause-risk dashboard and single-platform contract visibility Sirion lacks.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: This deal has exactly one substantive touchpoint on record (16 Sep 2025) and zero confirmed buyer engagement since, despite the deal owner sending seven follow-up emails over the following ten months. The pain articulated in that one call is genuine and well-quantified, but there is no champion, no economic buyer, and no second stakeholder thread to build on.

deal_risk.text: No economic buyer identified nearly a year in; without one, no one at Chemours has visible authority to actually select a platform.
The incumbent stack (SAP Ariba, JAGGAER, Sirion) remains fully functional enough that 'do nothing until the CET forms a project' is a live competing outcome.

win_position.text: No champion or economic buyer is mapped; the sole engaged contact, Timothy Duprey, told the rep on 16 Sep 2025 that he is 'not driving anything' on any potential project.

The buyer's own timeline is speculative: Timothy Duprey estimated a formal project 'maybe 2026... we'll see' with no charter or budget confirmed (16 Sep 2025), and nothing has updated that estimate in the ~10 months since.
The pain is well articulated and buyer-quantified: Timothy Duprey put Chemours' tail spend at '80 to 100 million' (16 Sep 2025), aligning with the $4-8M ANA savings case the rep presented.
Single-threaded: no second Chemours stakeholder has engaged since the opportunity was created on 16 Sep 2025, and zero Contact Roles are logged on the opportunity.

deal_momentum.text: No confirmed buyer response has been logged in the roughly 10 months since the 16 Sep 2025 call, despite the deal owner sending seven separate follow-ups (27 Oct 2025, 21 Jan 2026, 5 Feb 2026, 24 Mar 2026, 20 May 2026, 24 Jun 2026, 7 Jul 2026).
The most recent outreach, logged 7 Jul 2026, re-sends the same SAP Ariba/Sirion/JAGGAER discussion and Merlin Intake offer from 16 Sep 2025; no reply has been logged yet as of 9 Jul 2026.
The deal owner is actively re-engaging on a steady cadence rather than letting the account go untouched.

customer_commitment.text: Commitment extends no further than one exploratory call; Timothy Duprey positioned himself only as an interested stakeholder, explicitly not a decision-maker or driver.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: The one call on record shows strong directional fit between Zycus' pitch and the buyer's stated pain, but none of it has been validated beyond a single non-driving stakeholder.

hard.ais_why:
• Chemours category manager Timothy openly said he wants to understand how AI tools can help his role — a direct, customer-voice AI inquiry.
• Timothy framed AI as a way to catch up and work more efficiently, tying the interest to ongoing Ariba/S4HANA migration frustration.
• A formal project is only expected to form around 2026, so the AI conversation is real but the timing is forward; last activity 3/24/26.
hard.next_step: Hope you are having a great week.I’m following up on our previous discussion regarding Chemours' procurement environment and your ongoing landscape transition. We previously touched on the complexities of managing SAP Ariba, Sirion, and JAGGAER alongside your S/4 HANA journey, and how a unified suite could help streamline contract visibility and supplier risk.I wanted to check in and see how your project status and timeline are progressing, and if any new priorities have surfaced.As your team continues to navigate these systems, we would be glad to share a quick, tailored look at how our AI tools, like Merlin Intake, integrate smoothly with core ERP environments to simplify user adoption. If you or your team are open to a brief sync or a deep-dive on a specific module in the coming weeks, please let me know what your schedule looks like.

packets.subject: Timothy Duprey

value.name: SAP Ariba
value.quote: any progress at that time slowed or stopped... so now we're back and looking at, you know, the management portal of using Ariba with mainly a transactional level
value.how_we_win: Position the unified suite's deeper coverage (RFx, supplier risk, contract visibility) as solving what Ariba alone hasn't, independent of the stalled S/4HANA timeline.

value.name: SAP Ariba
value.quote: A prior Ariba modernization effort stalled when the S/4HANA migration paused
value.how_we_win: Position Zycus as the unified, AI-native platform that executes now while SAP's roadmap is uncertain; make Merlin Intake the Phase 1 quick-win tied to S/4HANA go-live, demonstrating faster time-to-value than restarting Ariba modernization.

value.name: SAP Ariba
value.quote: "Tail-spend leakage," poor catalog governance, and high off-contract purchases despite using Ariba P2P.
value.how_we_win: Position unified eProcurement + catalog governance to close the tail-spend and off-contract leakage Ariba has not solved.

value.name: SAP Ariba
value.quote: A lot of it was gonna depend on us migrating to S/4HANA... now we're back and looking at the management portal of using Ariba with mainly a transactional level.
value.how_we_win: Position the full source-to-pay suite (eProcurement/eInvoicing/Merlin Intake/ANA) as the modernization path that doesn't depend on the stalled S/4HANA migration.

packets.subject: SAP Ariba

value.name: JAGGAER
value.quote: we use YAGGER YAGGER for that or RFIs and RFRFQs and data gathering
value.how_we_win: Show how a single platform removes the need to split sourcing/RFx work across a second tool.

value.name: JAGGAER
value.quote: Managing SAP Ariba, Sirion, and JAGGAER as three disconnected platforms
value.how_we_win: Tail-spend plus core-sourcing in one platform with AI intake, vs JAGGAER as a separate tail-only tool.

value.name: Jaggaer
value.quote: Lack of leveraging Ariba for RFX space (using Jaggaer instead) and challenges with supplier risk and performance management (KPIs, analytics).
value.how_we_win: Bring RFX/sourcing into the same platform as P2P to eliminate the separate Jaggaer workflow and its disconnected supplier-performance data.

value.name: Jaggaer
value.quote: We use Jagger for that, or RFIs and RFQs and data gathering.
value.how_we_win: Show Zycus eSourcing/RFX automation consolidating Jaggaer's function onto the same platform as procurement and contracts.

packets.subject: Jaggaer

value.name: Sirion
value.quote: it doesn't give you the dashboards... you don't get an effective dashboard to, at a glance, give you enough of the collaborative quick glance information
value.how_we_win: Lead with the AI-driven clause-risk dashboard and single-platform contract visibility Sirion lacks.

value.name: Sirion
value.quote: Managing SAP Ariba, Sirion, and JAGGAER as three disconnected platforms
value.how_we_win: Consolidation story: replace Sirion CLM plus Ariba plus JAGGAER with one Zycus suite, eliminating integration tax and user friction.

value.name: Sirion
value.quote: Using a separate system (Sirion) for contract management, leading to separate databases and poor user experience/dashboards.
value.how_we_win: Consolidate contract lifecycle management into the same platform to remove the separate database and dashboard fragmentation Sirion causes.
